meta.title: The Frontier Macro
meta.subtitle: Rates, FX, the External Sector & Global Markets
meta.forAudience: For Quick Reference Only - Bangladesh & Global Markets
meta.headline:
Fiscal and financial sector developments remained in focus as the IMF intensified its assessment of Bangladesh's reform agenda. A key area of discussion was the government's ambitious FY27 revenue mobilisation plan, with the Fund scrutinising the assumptions behind the BDT 6.04tn target and evaluating whether the newly announced fiscal measures would be sufficient to achieve it. The review also extended to the country's external financing position, including debt repayment commitments, the cost and composition of foreign borrowings, the availability of concessional funding, and the implications of a higher share of variable-rate debt.

In a separate development, the Bangladesh Securities and Exchange Commission introduced a broad package of regulatory changes designed to revitalise the capital market. The reforms ease the path to public listing by simplifying the IPO process and creating greater scope for direct listings by financially sound companies. The regulator also relaxed margin lending rules by allowing financing against "B" category stocks, a policy shift intended to improve trading activity, deepen market liquidity, and strengthen overall investor confidence.

commentary.fx: BEPZA attracted unprecedented investment interest in FY26, receiving investment proposals worth USD 718 million, the highest in its history. The strong pipeline of commitments highlights sustained foreign investor confidence in Bangladesh's export processing zones despite an uncertain global economic backdrop.
commentary.equities:
The Bangladesh Securities and Exchange Commission (BSEC) has initiated a comprehensive reform of the IPO framework to make public listings more efficient and attractive. The proposed changes simplify regulatory requirements, expand the scope for direct listings by financially sound firms, and eliminate margin lending restrictions on "B" category shares, a move expected to improve market liquidity and broaden investor participation.

Grameenphone declared a 105% interim cash dividend for shareholders, even as its financial performance softened during the first half of the year. The telecom operator reported lower revenue and earnings compared with the corresponding period of the previous year.
commentary.global: Saudi Arabia has signaled stronger investment interest in Bangladesh's infrastructure sector, with a senior delegation indicating the possibility of investing up to USD 1 billion in strategic projects, including ports, railways, and other critical transport infrastructure, during discussions with Prime Minister Tarique Rahman.
commentary.rates: Bangladesh Bank has eased foreign borrowing regulations for wholly foreign-owned manufacturers by allowing them to obtain financing from parent companies, affiliated entities, and overseas shareholders under a general approval mechanism. The policy aims to improve access to offshore funding and strengthen the country's appeal as an FDI destination.
commentary.inflation: Consumer credit continued to expand during the January–March quarter of FY26, with outstanding loans surpassing BDT 1.58 trillion. The increase came despite persistently high inflation and elevated lending rates, indicating resilient household demand for credit.
commentary.external: The Invest Bangladesh Bill, 2026, has been approved by Parliament, clearing the way for the establishment of the Invest Bangladesh Authority. The new agency will consolidate the functions of BIDA, the PPP Authority, and BEZA into a single institution responsible for investment promotion and facilitation.
commentary.reserves: Bangladesh's external financial position strengthened significantly during FY26, as the financial account recorded a USD 4.16 billion surplus in the first eleven months, reversing a USD 214 million deficit recorded a year earlier. The improvement reflects a notable recovery in external capital inflows.
commentary.commodity: Bangladesh faces renewed challenges in securing long-term LNG supplies after Excelerate Energy reduced its contracted deliveries for 2026, following a similar move by QatarEnergy. With both suppliers cutting volumes, the country is expected to depend more heavily on higher-priced spot market purchases to meet domestic gas demand.

fx.text: BEPZA attracted unprecedented investment interest in FY26, receiving investment proposals worth USD 718 million, the highest in its history. The strong pipeline of commitments highlights sustained foreign investor confidence in Bangladesh's export processing zones despite an uncertain global economic backdrop.
The taka rose 0.52 to 123.37 per USD (interbank), with the BC sell rate at 123.75.

equities.text:
The Bangladesh Securities and Exchange Commission (BSEC) has initiated a comprehensive reform of the IPO framework to make public listings more efficient and attractive. The proposed changes simplify regulatory requirements, expand the scope for direct listings by financially sound firms, and eliminate margin lending restrictions on "B" category shares, a move expected to improve market liquidity and broaden investor participation.

Grameenphone declared a 105% interim cash dividend for shareholders, even as its financial performance softened during the first half of the year. The telecom operator reported lower revenue and earnings compared with the corresponding period of the previous year.

global.text: Saudi Arabia has signaled stronger investment interest in Bangladesh's infrastructure sector, with a senior delegation indicating the possibility of investing up to USD 1 billion in strategic projects, including ports, railways, and other critical transport infrastructure, during discussions with Prime Minister Tarique Rahman.
Term SOFR spans 3.73-3.99% across tenors, while EURIBOR sits lower at 2.48%+.
The 3-month SOFR-EURIBOR gap is 1.25pp, a live input for currency-of-borrowing decisions.

rates.text: Bangladesh Bank has eased foreign borrowing regulations for wholly foreign-owned manufacturers by allowing them to obtain financing from parent companies, affiliated entities, and overseas shareholders under a general approval mechanism. The policy aims to improve access to offshore funding and strengthen the country's appeal as an FDI destination.
The repo rate is 10.00%, the Bank Rate 4.00%.

ycurve.text: The local-currency curve is upward-sloping: the 91-day (9.17%) sits below the 10-year (10.24%).
Week-on-week the curve shifted down about 0.14pp on average.

inflation.text: Consumer credit continued to expand during the January–March quarter of FY26, with outstanding loans surpassing BDT 1.58 trillion. The increase came despite persistently high inflation and elevated lending rates, indicating resilient household demand for credit.

external.text: The Invest Bangladesh Bill, 2026, has been approved by Parliament, clearing the way for the establishment of the Invest Bangladesh Authority. The new agency will consolidate the functions of BIDA, the PPP Authority, and BEZA into a single institution responsible for investment promotion and facilitation.
Exports were $4.20bn against imports of $5.80bn - a trade gap of $1.60bn.

reserves.text: Bangladesh's external financial position strengthened significantly during FY26, as the financial account recorded a USD 4.16 billion surplus in the first eleven months, reversing a USD 214 million deficit recorded a year earlier. The improvement reflects a notable recovery in external capital inflows.
Gross reserves was little changed at $36.08bn (BPM6 $31.53bn).

commodity.text: Bangladesh faces renewed challenges in securing long-term LNG supplies after Excelerate Energy reduced its contracted deliveries for 2026, following a similar move by QatarEnergy. With both suppliers cutting volumes, the country is expected to depend more heavily on higher-priced spot market purchases to meet domestic gas demand.

special.title: Bangladesh Bank Eases External Borrowing Rules to Boost FDI and Industrial Investment
special.tag: Bangladesh Bank, External Borrowing, FDI, Foreign Investment, Offshore Loans, Foreign-Owned Firms, EPZ, Economic Zones, Industrial Investment
points: Bangladesh Bank has liberalised its external borrowing framework for wholly foreign-owned industrial enterprises to encourage greater foreign direct investment (FDI) and improve access to offshore funding.

Eligible companies can now obtain foreign loans from their parent companies, affiliated entities and overseas shareholders under a general authorisation mechanism, eliminating the need for prior case-by-case approval from the central bank.

The revised policy applies to fully foreign-owned industrial enterprises operating in Export Processing Zones (EPZs), Economic Zones (EZs), Hi-Tech Parks, as well as eligible manufacturing and service companies located outside these specialised zones.
Firms are permitted to access interest-free short-term loans with maturities of less than one year to finance working capital requirements.

Cost-bearing short-term external loans are also allowed, subject to an all-in financing cost ceiling of 3% per annum, with repayment on a bullet basis and rollover permitted for a cumulative tenor of up to three years.

Medium- and long-term foreign borrowings must be directed toward capital expenditure, including machinery imports, factory construction, technology acquisition and other eligible investment-related activities.
Trading companies remain excluded from the facility, with the revised framework intended specifically to support productive investment in industrial and service sectors.
The central bank expects the streamlined borrowing process to lower financing costs for foreign-owned businesses while reducing administrative hurdles associated with raising overseas funds.
By improving funding flexibility for multinational enterprises, the policy is designed to strengthen Bangladesh's competitiveness as an investment destination and support new FDI inflows.

The regulatory change forms part of Bangladesh Bank's broader strategy to facilitate private investment, enhance foreign capital inflows and support long-term industrial expansion through a more business-friendly external financing regime.
